summary: This notebook implements a production-ready inference pipeline for multi-class intracranial aneurysm detection. It standardizes variable-length DICOM series into fixed 32-slice inputs by applying modality-specific windowing and rescaling, then constructs a 3-channel image tensor from the middle slice, maximum intensity projection, and standard deviation projection. Patient metadata (age, sex) is normalized and fused with vision features through a custom multi-backbone architecture. The pipeline runs an ensemble of three pre-trained models with test-time augmentation and weighted averaging to generate robust predictions. Readers should learn how to handle heterogeneous medical imaging data, integrate tabular metadata with CNN/Transformer backbones, and structure a fault-tolerant inference server for Kaggle competitions.
competition_flow: Loads raw DICOM series, processes them into 3-channel image projections and patient metadata, runs predictions through a weighted ensemble of three pre-trained vision models with TTA, and outputs a submission DataFrame via the Kaggle inference server.
data_reading: Reads DICOM files using pydicom, extracts pixel arrays and metadata (modality, age, sex, rescale slope/intercept), and handles multi-frame/color images by converting to grayscale.
data_processing: Applies modality-specific DICOM windowing, rescales pixel values, resizes slices to 512x512, samples or pads to exactly 32 slices, creates 3-channel inputs (middle slice, MIP, std projection), normalizes metadata (age/100, sex 0/1), and applies Albumentations transforms including TTA (original, H-flip, V-flip, 90-rot).
features_engineering: Domain-derived image projections (middle slice, MIP, standard deviation across slices) and normalized patient metadata (age, sex).
ensembling: Weighted average of predictions from three models (EfficientNetV2: 0.4, ConvNeXt: 0.3, Swin: 0.3) with TTA applied per model.
insights: Variable-length DICOM series can be standardized by sampling or padding to a fixed slice count without losing critical anatomical context.
Combining spatial projections (MIP, std) with the middle slice provides complementary features for detecting subtle vascular anomalies.
Metadata (age, sex) can be effectively fused with vision features via a separate FC branch rather than raw concatenation.
TTA with geometric flips and rotations improves prediction stability for medical imaging tasks.
A production inference server requires robust error handling, memory cleanup, and fallback mechanisms to avoid Kaggle environment limits.
